one_line_takeaway: LLM-attacker uses a self-refining multi-agent LLM pipeline to pick which vehicles should attack an autonomous driving policy, generating harder safety-critical scenarios than TTC/kinetic-field heuristics and cutting the trained ADS's collision rate roughly in half.
key_finding: The LLM-identified attackers produce the most effective and among the most realistic adversarial scenarios, and adversarial training on them roughly halves the ADS collision rate. On attack success (collision ratio), LLM-attacker beats Random, Min-TTC, and Kinetic-Field identification across all attacker counts and both ego types (logged-replay and pretrained-RL ego). For adversarial training, the policy trained on two LLM attackers (LLM-II) achieves an 8.81% crash rate in normal test scenarios vs 22.61% for Replay-only training (about half), while LLM-I (one attacker) gives the best route completion. Two attackers are identified as the sweet spot between attack effectiveness and computational cost, since gains beyond two attackers are marginal.
core_contribution: The paper proposes LLM-attacker, a closed-loop adversarial-scenario-generation framework for autonomous driving systems (ADS) that uses coordinated LLM agents to solve two problems left open by prior adversarial-scenario work: (1) automatically identifying WHICH background vehicles should become 'attackers' (rather than manual/rule-based designation), and (2) feeding the generated safety-critical scenarios back into ADS training in a closed loop. The paper's own novelty is twofold: an agentic LLM 'Adversarial Vehicle Identifier' built from three specialized LLM modules (Initialization, Reflection, Modification) that iteratively generate and refine a Python risk-scoring function to pick the N most dangerous vehicles, and a closed-loop adversarial-training procedure where adversarial trajectories are recomputed as the ADS policy evolves. Note: the multi-attacker and closed-loop training idea builds explicitly on prior CAT [Zhang et al., ref 10], which the paper credits for the single/fixed-attacker closed-loop training baseline and the Bayes-factorized collision-probability formulation; the LLM-based identifier and the multi-attacker extension are this paper's own additions.
threat_model: The 'attacker' here is a red-team / stress-testing construct, not a cyber adversary: adversarial background vehicles in simulation are manipulated to induce collisions with an ADS-controlled ego vehicle, exposing safety-critical failure modes that are rare in logged real-world data. The adversary's capability is choosing which vehicles attack and optimizing their future trajectories to maximize collision probability with the ego vehicle (formalized as min over adversarial transition of the RL return, or equivalently max over adversarial trajectory of P(collision | scene context)). The purpose is dual-use/defensive: the same generated scenarios are used to adversarially train the ADS (max-min objective) to improve robustness. There is no assumption of network intrusion, sensor spoofing, or access to model internals; attackers act purely through vehicle motion in a MetaDrive simulation seeded from real Waymo scenarios.
limitations: Only a single small local LLM (LLaMA 3.1-8B) is tested; the paper itself flags comparing newer LLMs as future work. The identifier is slow: ~4.42 hours average per full run (2.10h LLM reasoning + 2.32h simulation validation), mitigated only by suggesting commercial APIs. Evaluation is entirely in the MetaDrive simulator on 485 WOMD scenarios with a single RL algorithm (TD3) as the ego ADS, so generalization to other ADS stacks and to real vehicles is unverified. Realism is only partially won: LLM-attacker has the lowest abnormal-jerk rate but Kinetic-Field has lower acceleration KL divergence, so LLM-attacker is not strictly the most realistic on every metric. Training results use only 3 seeds. No traffic-rule constraints are enforced on generated attacks (noted as future work), so some scenarios may be physically dangerous but not legally plausible. The collision-probability factorization assumes independence of vehicle futures, an approximation inherited from CAT.

datasets_used: Waymo Open Motion Dataset (WOMD) - 485 complex real-world traffic scenarios imported
Adversarial training split: 385 WOMD replay scenarios (+ their generated safety-critical variants) for training, 100 held-out WOMD normal scenarios (+ generated variants) for testing
quantitative_results: Attack success rate (collision ratio), one attacker, logged ego: LLM-attacker 63.54% vs Kinetic Field 57.71% vs Min TTC 54.17% vs Random 18.13%
Attack success rate, two attackers, logged ego: LLM-attacker 90.42% vs Kinetic Field 83.13% vs Min TTC 76.25% vs Random 38.96%
Attack success rate, one attacker, RL ego: LLM-attacker 45.00% vs Kinetic Field 37.50% vs Min TTC 37.92% vs Random 22.08%
Realism (Table II): LLM-attacker acceleration KL divergence 0.0112 and abnormal jerk rate 1.836% (lowest jerk); Kinetic Field lowest KL at 0.0075; Random worst (KL 0.0523, jerk 3.155%)
Adversarial training crash rate in normal test scenarios: LLM-II 8.81 +/- 1.64% vs Replay 22.61 +/- 5.12% vs Kinetic-II 19.50 +/- 3.44% vs LLM-I 13.28 +/- 1.89% (LLM-II roughly halves Replay)
Adversarial training route completion (normal): LLM-I best at 78.61 +/- 0.37%, LLM-II 75.37%, Replay 74.48%, Kinetic-II 61.93%
Two-attacker training, crash rate: LLM-II 23.79% vs Replay 45.07%
Identifier convergence: three rounds each ended with at least 83% attack success rate; best at iteration 11 of Round 1; average run time 4.42 hours (2.10h LLM reasoning + 2.32h simulation)
Per-scenario attack computational cost grows with attacker count: 0.197s (1), 0.263s (2), 0.377s (3), 0.450s (4), 0.535s (5)
baselines_compared: Random (adversarial vehicles chosen randomly from background vehicles)
Min TTC (vehicles with minimum time-to-collision to ego)
Replay training (real-world WOMD replay scenarios only, no generated adversarial scenarios) - training baseline
Kinetic-II (two attackers identified by Kinetic Field) - training baseline
Ego-agent variants used for evaluation: Log agent (real logged trajectory replay) and RL agent (pretrained TD3 policy)
